text:
The phenomenon of Czech university students remaining at the same level of language competence, which the authors label as the “lack of a qualitative leap,” represents a serious issue in contemporary English as a Foreign Language teaching and learning in the Czech Republic. In many cases, learners are unable to progress to the more advanced levels of competence required in their academic and future professional careers. The present study, based on the empirical research of students studying English at university level, examines the effects of conventional teaching methods such as the Grammar-Translation Method and the PPP method. According to the authors, these, as well as other conventional methods, are often insufficient to generate the essential cognitive change necessary for the development of the higher level of language competence.

The authors argue for a change to a more process-oriented pedagogy that makes confidence, creativity, reflection, experimentation and accountability its foundational “changing agents”. The teaching strategy at the heart of this redefinition involves training students for small group interaction by using Tuckman’s process of small group development. Results from two surveys seem to show that language learners’ greatest obstacles lie in the psychological domain: Students have low self-esteem, which stems from two sources: lack of confidence in speaking due to apprehension over making grammatical and other types of mistakes, and a general belief that language learning is a process best carried out under duress of some kind. The authors argue for student autonomy and greater psychological involvement in the language learning process as alternatives to an over-reliance on drill-based pedagogies in which students get “stuck” in repeating the same language structures and sentences for years on end with no apparent or visible progress and in need therefore of a more empowering approach to language learning.
